speaker: interviewer
text: --survivor Alexander Kronhart, reel 3. Now Judge, in the course of these trials, were any women defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were several women, because we had women and even young girls tried for shooting American soldiers. And when it came to sentence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took into consideration their youth. Like a 14-year-old girl, the other two colonels on the bench, they said to execute her. I said, no way. She's 14. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I don't think that it's fair to sentence her to death. I vote against it. And the other two changed their minds.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sentence did she get for shooting at American soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave her five years, five years in prison. And in another case, you know, sometimes I would be able to correct the [GERMAN], the translator. Like one witness was testifying where they killed some American soldier. The witness said, [GERMAN]. I had no weapon. And the translator, the [GERMAN], when he translated he said, I had a weapon. I says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I had no weapon. I had to correct the [GERMAN], the German [GERMAN]. And then we had cases-- oh, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One defendant, he tried to escape. So he opened the door and he said he looked to the right and to the left, and he said, the coast is clear. And the [GERMAN], the translator, he said, the-- the air is clean. I said, just a minute. That's an expression. [GERMAN]. It means the coast is clear. It doesn't mean that the weather is clear. There were things like that that happened all the time. So-- 'cause I learned my German in the courtroom. And I was able to follow the translator very closely. And that was important, because sometimes they made mistakes. And there was nobody else to correct them, so I had to correct them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are the-- are your court records now in Washington? Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I assume that the-- the Pentagon might have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I assume they would be-- the transcripts would now be in Washington, in the archives.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I don't know whether-- first, they went to the Appeals Court. And-- and there was I think, 3 or 4 judges there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I imagine if you wanted to look up the court records, they must be on microfilm in the-- in the Pentagon probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: They never had microfilm in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but they would be subsequently microfilmed to save space.

speaker: interviewee
text: I doubt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't think so?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was all done by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Court stenographers.

speaker: interviewee
text: --stenographers. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not like today, where they have computers and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing like that today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your work with the court then ended in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: '49 or early '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we built a house in Island Park, Harbor Isle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You came back to the United States in early 1950, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we found some land--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --right at a-- on the water. And we built a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you choose Island Park?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good question. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I came back, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a German manufacturer of disks, disks, grinding wheels. And he learned about my being in Germany. And he used to come to America. He used to send-- sell his patents to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to think of the names-- manufacturing companies here in the States. But he found me. And he called me and he visited me. And he was going to the different manufacturers and he wanted to do business in the States. So he found me and we became good friends. In fact, we had him-- yeah. So while we were-- and we took him to concerts in Jones Beach. That's near Long Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then we learned about some land that was for sale. So we looked at it and we liked it. And you know who owned that land? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He was the commissioner of parks in the New York area. He owned that land. Not Robert Moses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert Moses. And he had 10 lots for sale. And next to him, there were three lots. And there was a police lieutenant that owned those lots. He used to come by boat. And Robert Moses, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what he did, he had some city workers come to his land to clean up the land, mow the lawn, and so on. And this retired lieutenant, police lieutenant, he squealed on him. So Robert Moses decided to sell the 10 lots. He was going to build a house there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we learned about it. All this is fate, you know? And so we came along and I offered him, like, $400 a lot. You know what they sell for now? Like $20,000 a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we built a house on it. And he visited. And he slept there. And he used to come. And we represented him. And he was-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] he was arrested in Germany, because he once said Hitler is going to lose the war. They arrested him, but they didn't execute him. They kept him in prison for a while until he got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? In the '30s? Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would be like-- would be like, yeah, before the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or before the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He made those remarks during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, during the war. Before America had entered the war, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. America was at war already. So he made a remark--

speaker: interviewer
text: But how could he be in Germany if America was at war.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Germany. He was a German. He was a manufacturer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you mean this man. Oh, you're not talking-- I thought you were talking about Moses.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You didn't give his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have his name somewhere, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. He thought that Hitler would lose the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he-- somebody heard. You know, there one word and everybody knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was put in prison for that. But then somehow-- he was a very successful businessman. Somehow he got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let him off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then after the war, he-- he wanted to do business in America, so that's why he came and he found me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, OK. Yes. So you remained there in Island Park and you had your practice there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a practice here in zoning, variances, real estate sales. And also I became the attorney for the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SNEEZES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Bless you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I became the attorney for the school. And we did a lot of good work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you essentially had a suburban law practice then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Until 1970, I came to Key Biscayne. We retired. And then from Key Biscayne we came-- no. Key Biscayne, then my wife started to get ill. And she finally passed away in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October 31, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: 60. And by the way, this is her. This is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I show her to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if you can get this on tape, but-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] this is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will get it afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get it afterwards, yes. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she passed away. And then I found this place. I didn't want to live alone, so I found this place. And I like the people and I like the way they conduct it. And I'm very happy here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on your legal-- legal career, and particularly your work as a United States judge, what kind of conclusions have you drawn from that? You mentioned fate a great deal, and destiny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Fate and destiny, because I think my whole life was fate. And meeting my wife, that's how I really-- and then meeting Mickey Marcus, and then meeting Judge Lucius Clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Colonel Marcus took part in the Israel War of Independence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was killed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben-Gurion got him to become-- he was a graduate of West Point, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a colonel. Ben-Gurion, during the '46 war, or '48 war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The '48 war.

speaker: interviewee
text: '48 war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Independence war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he got Mickey Marcus to go to Israel to fight. And he became a general.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, Mickey Marcus was taking a walk, and the Israeli soldier said in Hebrew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: who goes there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mickey Marcus didn't understand. He didn't answer. And that's why the Israeli soldier shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. He was killed-- friendly fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They made a picture,

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Yes. That was made, I think, in the '50s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quite a few years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was a great guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you knew him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was the one that got me to meet Lucius Clay, General Clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Destiny.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see, that's why I say it's all fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took part in historic events.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including--

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, I say it's all like a dream to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean it seems unreal now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't believe that it happened to me. It could have happened to anybody. But I still think it's the ESP-- Extrasensatory--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me take you back to Washington when you were with the OPA--

speaker: interviewee
text: OPA. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --an the Labor Relations Board.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware of the Holocaust and what Hitler was doing to the European Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you become aware of that, though? What year? America entered the war in December, on December the 7th, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when did public-- the public become aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I read about this even before the war started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Churchill, Roosevelt, and who was the Russian there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Stalin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stalin. They knew what was going on. You know, they gathered information. And they met several times. But then ultimately, in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have the date here. They met in Yalta, Russia. And that's when it was decided to try them all as war crimes defendants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I visited Russia in 1984. And I visited Yalta. And they have that big photograph with Roosevelt, Stalin, and Churchill sitting and so on. I wanted to see where they-- that was determined then, that they were going to try them all for their war crimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, though, what public protests and what public actions took place in America against the Nazis that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roosevelt had enough trouble because, there was a depression going on in '32, '33. And there was nothing that they could do about it, because we had to deal with the Depression. Banks were being closed. There was a bank holiday. So we weren't able to have all these meetings to decide what to do with Germany. We weren't even in the war yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were in Washington, did you receive any reports or-- or get any inside information about the massacres in the ghettos and the death camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't get any. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they were, nobody was able to do anything about it. We weren't even in the war yet. And they were busy with the Depression. They were thinking about drafts and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to Washington, though, there were public protests at Madison Square Garden, weren't there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- can you recall any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do, because I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we had our office in Wall Street, I remember I was getting on a bus and one guy was a-- a Nazi. He's hollering heil Hitler. And I got up. I wanted to punch him in the nose. But he got off the bus. He ran away. We knew what was going on, but you couldn't do a damn thing about it, because the US was having its own problems. The Depression was going. The banks were closing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until later-- later, when Roosevelt was able to get together with Stalin and Churchill and have these meetings to decide what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- the constitution of the War Refugee Board? Roosevelt set up the War Refugee Board, but that wasn't until 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You do not recall that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't-- I was busy with the courts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course. Of course. Anyway, do you have any particular message that you'd like to leave arising out of your own judicial experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would say that this kind of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] cruel [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] treatment of humanity that the Germans had, killing six million Jews, should not happen again. It has to be stopped right at the beginning. If anything comes up again like this, I think we'll be better prepared for it knowing what the Holocaust ended. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was the worst part of history of any war. In all history, such a catastrophe never happened as this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you done much reading about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Unfortunately, the-- I knew about it. I visited Dachau. I visited Belsenberg. I visited [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all-- most of the death camps. I saw the ovens and I saw what went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Auschwitz too, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When was that? After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, after the war, yeah. I didn't get there 'til after.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after the war, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: I visited there in-- during the time that I was sitting as a judge already. I went to visit. I went to Dachau. In fact, I brought my-- some employees, German employees, stenographers, who lived near Dachau. And they said they didn't know a thing about it. But they knew, because the screams that went on and the smells and everything. They should have known. They knew. They knew. This is unheard of in all history. It was just a catastrophe. And I just hope it never happens again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the International Military Tribunal at least administered justice to some of the major criminals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They were hanged. They weren't shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were hanged. Some were acquitted too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the others got some prison sentences, like Hess. By the way, I met Hess in the prison. You know, he flew to England to try to get England to stop the war so they could get after Russia, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually met him, then? What do you remember of the meeting with Hess?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, England wasn't getting out of the war. They knew there was a war going on, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. No, but you met Hess, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I met him in prison later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was 6 feet, 7 feet tall. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any of your conversation with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just it was very informal. He was a prisoner there. And do you know, I think he was the only prisoner left in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think a-- an American soldier finally shot him. They say he died from-- you know, I think he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The circumstances of his death are still not clear.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was found-- he was found asphyxiated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then I think it was done by somebody, because they didn't want to continue that prison. He was the only prisoner there for a number of years.

speaker: interviewer
text: He lived to be in his 90s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I visited him before I came back to the States. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] But if that's the only mistake you made, that's pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She could have been 92.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] And I attended a retreat [INAUDIBLE] almost 20 years ago. Yitzchak Greenberg, this rabbi from New York, came out to Arizona. And he taught us that we had the power to-- to bomb the railways leading to the concentration camps. And they chose not [AUDIO OUT] For many years, and [INAUDIBLE] in the plain view of family. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Go ahead. The one on the left, I'm being sworn in by Judge Clark. He was formerly the head of the Court of Appeals of the State of New Jersey. And he was the head judge in Nuremberg. The middle one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, wait. Let me film a little more on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'll ask you when we're finished. OK. Go ahead. The middle one. Yeah. And that was in early 1946. I don't remember the exact date. The one in the middle in the front row, there's 1, 2, 3, 4, and I'm in the second one, being sworn in. That was at the same time. And this is the rest of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the way it is with cameras. Go head.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Which one do you want now?

speaker: interviewer
text: That one, the last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last one. The last one are the rest of the judges. It's the same time, but this is a different angle, you see, because I'm down here, down at this end. That's the same as me being here. And these are the rest of the judges that were being sworn in at the same time. That's early '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken, Judge?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a photograph, I would say, in early '46. We wore uniforms in the courtroom at that time. But later on, we wore civilian clothes. This is an early '46 photograph.
